faq.answer: After clashing with Hegel during his university lectures, he despised the cliques of university professors and continued his independent research without being tied to any institution.

faq.question: How did Schopenhauer evaluate Kant's philosophy?
faq.answer: While accepting Kant's ideas critically, he was convinced that he correctly inherited Kant's philosophy, and he praised Kant's discovery of the a priori nature of cognition as a great historical achievement.

faq.question: What did Schopenhauer see as the basis of ethics?
faq.answer: Unlike Kant, who saw reason and law as the foundation of morality, Schopenhauer proposed compassion as the basis of morality and explained it metaphysically.

timeline.event: Born in Woolsthorpe, Lincolnshire, England; his father, Isaac Newton Sr., died in October 1642, before he was born.
When Newton was three, his mother, Hannah Ayscough, remarried, and he was left in the care of his maternal grandmother.
His mother returned after his stepfather died.
Sent to The King's School in Grantham to begin classical education; boarded with an apothecary named Mr. Clark, where he showed an interest in chemistry.
His mother called him back to Woolsthorpe to teach him farming.
Admitted to Trinity College, Cambridge.
Generalized the binomial theorem; when Cambridge University closed due to the Great Plague, he returned home for two years.
Returned to Cambridge University when it reopened and earned his Master's degree.
Built the reflecting telescope.
Succeeded Professor Isaac Barrow as Lucasian Professor of Mathematics at Cambridge University and began his research on calculus.
Elected a Fellow of the Royal Society, and developed his theory of light based on his observation of a prism separating white light into the visible spectrum.
Discovered the interference phenomenon in thin films.
Published Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica, presenting the foundations of classical mechanics and universal gravitation, and proving the three laws of motion.
Served as a Member of Parliament.
Served as the Master of the Royal Mint.
Became President of the Royal Society of London following the death of Robert Hooke.
Knighted by Queen Anne.
Devised an experiment to verify the existence of the aether.
Died in his sleep at the age of 84, and his remains were interred in Westminster Abbey.

keyAchievements.title: Development of Calculus
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to calculus alongside Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz, and created Newton's method for finding the roots of a function.

keyAchievements.title: Generalization of the Binomial Theorem
keyAchievements.description: Proved the generalized binomial theorem and contributed to the study of power series.

keyAchievements.title: Classical Mechanics and Universal Gravitation
keyAchievements.description: Provided the foundations of classical mechanics and universal gravitation and formulated Newton's laws of motion in Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of the Reflecting Telescope
keyAchievements.description: Built the first practical reflecting telescope.

keyAchievements.title: Theory of Light
keyAchievements.description: Developed the theory of light based on his observation of a prism separating white light into the visible spectrum.

keyAchievements.title: Newton's Law of Cooling
keyAchievements.description: Formulated Newton's law of cooling through experiments, studied the speed of sound, and introduced the concept of a Newtonian fluid.

keyAchievements.title: Inverse-Square Law of Gravity
keyAchievements.description: Discovered the inverse-square law of gravity, which states that the gravitational attraction between two bodies is inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.

faq.question: Who was Isaac Newton?
faq.answer: Isaac Newton was an English mathematician, physicist, and astronomer.

faq.question: What is Newton's most influential book?
faq.answer: Published in 1687, Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica established the foundations of classical mechanics and universal gravitation, and is considered one of the most influential books in the history of science.

faq.question: How did Newton contribute to calculus?
faq.answer: He contributed to calculus alongside Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz and discovered Newton's method for approximating the roots of a function.

faq.question: What are Newton's laws of motion?
faq.answer: Newton's laws of motion consist of three laws: the law of inertia, the law of acceleration, and the law of action and reaction.

faq.question: How did Newton explain gravity?
faq.answer: He revealed the inverse-square law of gravity and explained the law of universal gravitation, showing that the force that pulls an apple to the ground is the same force that keeps the moon in its orbit.

faq.question: Did Newton ever marry?
faq.answer: He was engaged in his youth, but he was so devoted to his research and work that it did not lead to marriage, and he remained single his entire life.

faq.question: What were Newton's religious beliefs?
faq.answer: He rejected the Trinity but was deeply religious; he believed in a single Creator and reportedly spent a great deal of time on biblical interpretation.

faq.question: What public offices did Newton hold?
faq.answer: He held public roles as a Member of Parliament, Master of the Royal Mint, and President of the Royal Society of London.

timeline.event: Born in Shrewsbury, England.
Receives primary and secondary education.
Begins studying at Shrewsbury School.
Begins studying medicine at the University of Edinburgh.
Leaves the University of Edinburgh and enrolls in Christ's College, Cambridge to study theology.
Graduates from Cambridge with top honors and boards the HMS Beagle on December 27.
Completes the Beagle expedition.
The Beagle arrives in Falmouth, Cornwall.
His first paper on the uplift of the South American continent is read to the Geological Society of London, and he presents mammal and bird specimens to the Zoological Society.
Elected to the Council of the Geological Society.
Moves to London to join Charles Lyell's scientific circles.
Ponders 'the possibility of one species changing into another' in his Red Notebook.
Elected Secretary of the Geological Society.
Marries Emma Wedgwood and publishes The Voyage of the Beagle.
Retires to the outskirts of London to collect and organize data on evolution.
Publishes The Structure and Distribution of Coral Reefs, his first biological monograph.
Writes a 35-page pencil sketch of his theory of evolution by natural selection.
Publishes Geological Observations on the Volcanic Islands.
Publishes Geological Observations on South America based on his Beagle expedition data.
Begins writing his theory of evolution.
Presents his theory alongside a paper by Alfred Russel Wallace at the Linnean Society.
Publishes On the Origin of Species, establishing the fact of biological evolution and 'natural selection'.
Publishes The Descent of Man, which deals with human evolution.
Publishes The Expression of the Emotions in Man and Animals.
Publishes The Formation of Vegetable Mould through the Action of Worms.
Dies at the age of 73 and is buried in Westminster Abbey.

keyAchievements.title: Greatest Contribution to the Theory of Evolution
keyAchievements.description: As an English biologist and geologist, made the greatest contribution to evolutionary theory.

keyAchievements.title: Introduction of Natural Selection Theory
keyAchievements.description: Introduced natural selection occurring through the struggle for survival in a joint paper with Alfred Russel Wallace.

keyAchievements.title: Writing On the Origin of Species and Presenting Evolutionary Theory
keyAchievements.description: Wrote On the Origin of Species in 1859, presenting the theory of evolution with compelling evidence.

keyAchievements.title: Providing a Unifying Theory of the Life Sciences
keyAchievements.description: Darwin's scientific discoveries form a unifying theory in the life sciences, explaining the diversity of life.

keyAchievements.title: Challenging Creationism
keyAchievements.description: Proposed the theory of biological evolution in On the Origin of Species, overturning the dominant creationist view of the time and opening a new era.

keyAchievements.title: Conviction in Biological Evolution Through the Beagle Voyage
keyAchievements.description: Became convinced of biological evolution after meticulously observing geology, flora, and fauna during the Beagle expedition.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of Major Works
keyAchievements.description: Left numerous academic works including The Structure and Distribution of Coral Reefs (1842), Geological Observations on the Volcanic Islands (1844), Geological Observations on South America (1846), and The Descent of Man (1871).

faq.question: When was Charles Darwin born and when did he die?
faq.answer: Charles Darwin was born on February 12, 1809, and died on April 19, 1882.

faq.question: What is Charles Darwin's most important achievement?
faq.answer: Charles Darwin made the greatest contribution to the theory of evolution and established natural selection with the publication of On the Origin of Species in 1859.

faq.question: What was Charles Darwin's stance on slavery?
faq.answer: Charles Darwin opposed slavery, having been outraged by the white enslavement of black people during his travels in South America.

faq.question: What was the purpose of Charles Darwin boarding the Beagle?
faq.answer: Initially, he boarded as a civilian companion to satisfy the captain's intellectual desire for association with a gentleman, but he later acted as a naturalist as his exceptional skills in natural history were recognized.

faq.question: Is the rumor true that Charles Darwin changed his religious beliefs in his later years?
faq.answer: The rumor that Darwin accepted the biblical creation story as truth and recovered his faith in Jesus Christ before he died is false.

timeline.event: Claude Monet is born on November 14 in the 9th arrondissement of Paris.
Baptized Oscar-Claude on May 20 at the Notre-Dame de Lorette.
His family moves to Le Havre in Normandy.
Enrolls in the Le Havre secondary school of the arts on April 1.
His mother dies.
Meets fellow painter Eugène Boudin.
Arrives in Paris in April and settles at the Hotel 'du Nouveau Monde'.
Enrolls in the Académie Suisse, run by Charles Suisse, early in the year (around February) and meets Camille Pissarro.
Drafted into the army in Le Havre on March 2 and joins the First Regiment of African Light Cavalry on April 29.
Contracts typhoid fever in the spring, returns to Le Havre to recover, and meets Johan Jongkind in the summer.
Returns to Paris in December, enrolls in the studio of Charles Gleyre, and meets Auguste Renoir and Frédéric Bazille.
Successfully debuts at the Salon with The Point of la Hève at Low Tide and Mouth of the Seine at Honfleur.
Submits The Woman in the Green Dress and Pavé de Chailly to the Salon and is accepted.
His then-mistress Camille Doncieux gives birth to their first son, Jean.
Marries Camille on June 28, and takes refuge in London and the Netherlands just before the outbreak of the Franco-Prussian War.
His father dies, and the family moves to Argenteuil.
Moves to the 'pink house with green shutters' in Argenteuil, and exhibits Impression, Sunrise at the First Impressionist Exhibition.
Paints Woman with a Parasol - Madame Monet and Her Son.
Camille Monet falls seriously ill; another exhibition is organized in opposition to the Salon.
Exhibits seven paintings of the Gare Saint-Lazare at the Third Impressionist Exhibition on April 5.
His second son Michel is born, the family moves to the village of Vétheuil, and Camille is diagnosed with uterine cancer.
Camille dies.
Submits two paintings to the Salon, and one is accepted.
Moves to Poissy in December with Alice Hoschedé, the wife of Ernest Hoschedé, and her children.
Participates in his last, the Seventh Impressionist Exhibition.
Moves to a rented house in Giverny with the children on April 29, and his first major retrospective is held.
Goes on a short painting trip to the Italian Riviera and Genoa with Auguste Renoir in December.
Stays in Bordighera from January 18 to April 5, producing 38 paintings.
Travels to the Netherlands to paint tulips.
Meets John Singer Sargent in the summer.
Purchases the house in Giverny.
15 paintings of the Haystacks series are exhibited at the Galerie Durand-Ruel.
Paints 26 views of Rouen Cathedral.
Exhibits 20 paintings of Rouen Cathedral.
61 paintings are exhibited at the Galerie Georges Petit.
Begins painting water lilies, and stays in London for six weeks.
Exhibits the first garden paintings, primarily focusing on the Japanese bridge.
Expands the pond at his home; in February, an exhibition of the Water Lilies series in New York City achieves great success.
Increases the size of the water garden to nearly 4,000 square meters.
The series 'Views of the Thames in London - 1900-1904' is exhibited in May and June.
Spends the autumn at the Palazzo Barbaro in Venice with Alice.
An exhibition titled 'Water Lilies' opens on May 6, featuring 48 paintings.
His second wife Alice dies.
Travels to London to consult with the German ophthalmologist Richard Liebreich.
His eldest son Jean dies.
Begins work on a series of decorative paintings for the French government.
Begins a series of landscape paintings 'at full strength'.
Prescription for mydriatic drops temporarily alleviates his vision problems.
Undergoes cataract surgery.
With improved vision, begins retouching some of the works produced before surgery.
Dies of lung cancer at the age of 86 on December 5 and is buried in the Giverny church cemetery.
27 panel paintings are exhibited at the Musée de l'Orangerie in May.
André Masson's essay helps shift the perception of Monet's paintings.
Water Lily paintings held by the Museum of Modern Art in New York are destroyed in a fire.
Monet's house, garden, and water lily pond are bequeathed by Michel to the French Academy of Fine Arts.
Monet's garden is restored and opened to the public.
A Google Doodle is created on November 14 to commemorate Claude Monet's 161st birthday.
The Houses of Parliament, Sunlight Effect in the Fog is sold for $20.1 million.
A paper is published in the Proceedings of the Royal Society identifying the locations where the London paintings were created.
Cliffs Near Dieppe is stolen for the second time in August.
The Railway Bridge at Argenteuil is sold for $41.4 million on May 6.
Cliffs Near Dieppe is recovered in June.
Le Bassin Aux Nymphéas is sold for £40,921,250 (approx. $80,451,178) on June 24.
The Church at Vétheuil and Le Bassin Aux Nymphéas become the subject of a court case in New York in October.
A Monet painting formerly owned by a Jewish retailer is discovered in the suitcase of Cornelius Gurlitt.
The Nazi-looted artwork On the Seashore is restituted to the granddaughters of the Parlagi couple.

keyAchievements.title: Founding and Pioneering Impressionism
keyAchievements.description: Known as the 'father of Impressionism', he is considered a founder and pioneer of the movement and a key precursor to Modernism.

keyAchievements.title: Origin of the Term Impressionism
keyAchievements.description: The term 'Impressionism' originated from the title of his painting Impression, Sunrise, exhibited at the First Impressionist Exhibition in 1874.

keyAchievements.title: Consistent Practice of En Plein Air Landscape Painting
keyAchievements.description: As the most consistent practitioner of the Impressionist philosophy over a long career, he expressed his perception of nature in plein air landscape paintings.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneering the Series Method
keyAchievements.description: Pioneered the series method of painting the same scene many times to capture changes in light and the passing of seasons, with major works including the Haystacks, Rouen Cathedral, and Water Lilies series.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of the Giverny Garden
keyAchievements.description: Purchased a house and land in Giverny, northern France, and began a vast landscaping project including a water lily pond, which became the greatest source of inspiration for his art.

keyAchievements.title: Influence on Modern Art
keyAchievements.description: His late works produced in Giverny under the influence of cataracts are considered to have provided 'major' inspiration for 20th-century art and modern abstract art.

faq.question: Who was Claude Monet?
faq.answer: Claude Monet was a French Impressionist painter, a founder and pioneer of the Impressionist movement, often referred to as the 'father of Impressionism'.

faq.question: Where did the term 'Impressionism' originate?
faq.answer: The term 'Impressionism' originated from the title of his painting Impression, Sunrise, exhibited at the First Impressionist Exhibition in 1874.

faq.question: What are Monet's representative series paintings?
faq.answer: His representative series include the Haystacks, Rouen Cathedral, and the Water Lilies, which captivated him for the last 20 years of his life.

faq.question: Where was Monet primarily active?
faq.answer: He lived in Giverny, northern France, from 1883, where he purchased a house and land and began a vast landscaping project that included his water lily pond.

faq.question: How did Monet's eyesight affect his work?
faq.answer: Cataracts made color perception increasingly difficult, leading his paintings to become broader and more abstract; after surgery, his paintings tended to have stronger blue tones than before.

faq.question: How did Monet's work influence modern art?
faq.answer: His late works produced in Giverny under the influence of cataracts are considered to have provided 'major' inspiration to 20th-century art and modern abstract art.

timeline.event: Born as the third son of Léopold Hugo in Besançon, eastern France.
Fascinated by reading and writing poetry at the Cordeliers boarding school in Paris, vowing to become a great French writer.
Published his first poetry collection, Odes et poésies diverses, and got married.
Published the novel Han d'Islande.
Awarded the Knight of the Legion of Honour by the French royal family in recognition of his merits as a writer.
Published the poetry collection Odes et Ballades and the novel Bug-Jargal.
Directly opposed classical literature and led romantic writers through the publication of the preface to his play Cromwell.
Published Les Orientales and Le Dernier jour d'un condamné.
The "Battle of Hernani" broke out between the romantic and classical schools around the premiere of the play Hernani.
Published the novel Notre-Dame de Paris, the poetry collection Les Feuilles d'automne, and the play Marion Delorme.
Published the play Le roi s'amuse.
Published the plays Lucrèce Borgia and Marie Tudor.
Published the poetry collection Les Chants du crépuscule.
Published the poetry collection Les Voix intérieures.
Published the verse play Ruy Blas.
Published the poetry collection Les Rayons et les Ombres.
Became a member of the Académie Française.
The play Les Burgraves was severely criticized, and his eldest daughter Léopoldine and her husband died in a boat capsizing accident.
Devoted himself to political activities, barely publishing any works.
Solidified his humanitarian tendencies, stimulated by surrounding circumstances including the February Revolution.
Rebelled against Napoleon III's coup d'état, was exiled abroad, and began about 19 years of exile.
Published the poetry collection Les Châtiments.
Published the poetry collection Les Contemplations.
Published the first series of the poetry collection La Légende des siècles.
Published the novel Les Misérables.
Published the novel Les Travailleurs de la mer.
Wrote Les Années funestes, depicting the reality of French workers.
Returned to Paris as a defender of republicanism with the fall of Napoleon III due to the Franco-Prussian War.
Published L'Année terrible.
Published the novel Quatrevingt-treize.
Published the second series of La Légende des siècles.
Wrote his will.
Published the third series of La Légende des siècles.
Died, and his remains were interred in the Panthéon in a state funeral.

keyAchievements.title: Leading Romantic Literature
keyAchievements.description: He presented the theory of romantic theater in the preface to his play Cromwell, won the "Battle of Hernani", and led romantic writers.

keyAchievements.title: Writing Masterpiece Novels
keyAchievements.description: He solidified his position as a novelist with Notre-Dame de Paris and wrote numerous masterpieces including Les Misérables during his exile.

keyAchievements.title: Social Criticism and Humanitarianism
keyAchievements.description: Through his works, he expressed love for poor workers, emphasized popular art, and solidified his humanitarian tendencies.

keyAchievements.title: Major Figure in the French Literary World
keyAchievements.description: He occupied an important position in the French literary world, receiving the Knight of the Legion of Honour in 1825 and becoming a member of the Académie Française in 1841.

faq.question: Who was Victor Hugo?
faq.answer: He was a French poet, novelist, and playwright.

faq.question: What impact did Victor Hugo have on romanticism?
faq.answer: He presented the theory of romantic theater through the preface to his play Cromwell, won the "Battle of Hernani", and led romantic writers.

faq.question: What are Victor Hugo's representative works?
faq.answer: They include the novels Notre-Dame de Paris and Les Misérables.

faq.question: Why was Victor Hugo in exile?
faq.answer: He rebelled against Napoleon III's coup d'état in 1851, was exiled abroad, and began about 19 years of exile.

faq.question: What was Victor Hugo's relationship with Catholicism?
faq.answer: His works were banned by the Catholic Church, and he could not reconcile with Catholicism, even refusing the church's prayers.

faq.question: When did Victor Hugo die, and where is he buried?
faq.answer: He died on May 22, 1885, and his remains were interred in the Panthéon.

timeline.event: Born into an artisan family in the Katsushika district of Edo (Childhood name: Tokitaro).
Began drawing around age 6.
Worked at a bookstore and lending library at age 12.
Worked as an apprentice woodcarver at age 14.
Entered the studio of Katsukawa Shunshō at age 18.
Received the name Shunrō from his master and published his first series of prints of Kabuki actors.
Produced Fireworks at Ryōgoku Bridge in Edo.
Death of his master Katsukawa Shunshō.
Second marriage.
Passed the name Tawaraya Sōri to his pupil, began working as an independent artist, and used the name "Hokusai Tomisa".
Began using the name "Katsushika Hokusai", published the landscape print series Famous Views of the Eastern Capital and Eight Views of Edo, and started taking on pupils.
Painted a massive portrait of the Buddhist monk Daruma, about 200 square meters in size, during an Edo festival.
Collaborated with popular novelist Kyokutei Bakin to produce a series of illustrated novels.
Produced illustrations for the fantasy novel Strange Tales of the Crescent Moon.
Changed his name to Taito at age 51 and began producing Hokusai Manga and various etehon (art manuals).
Produced Quick Lessons in Simplified Drawing.
The first volume of Hokusai Manga was published and became a huge success.
Painted a giant portrait of Daruma outside the Hongan-ji Nagoya Betsuin in Nagoya.
Changed his name to Iitsu and began gaining a solid reputation as an artist throughout Japan.
Produced his masterpiece, Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji.
Produced Oceans of Wisdom (Shika Shashin-kyō).
Began working under the name "Gakyō Rōjin Manji" (The Old Man Mad About Art) and produced One Hundred Views of Mount Fuji.
Produced his last print series, One Hundred Poems Explained by the Nurse.
A fire destroyed his studio and much of his work.
Visited Obuse in Shinano Province at age 83 and stayed there for several years.
Painted Chinese lions (shishi) every morning as a talisman to ward off bad luck.
Painted Dragon Flying in Clouds above Mount Fuji and Tiger in Snow.
Died and was buried at Seikyō-ji Temple in Taito Ward, Tokyo.

keyAchievements.title: Production of Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji
keyAchievements.description: He produced this ukiyo-e series, which includes the iconic The Great Wave off Kanagawa, earning immense fame both domestically and internationally.

keyAchievements.title: Expanding Ukiyo-e Subjects
keyAchievements.description: He played a crucial role in broadening the subjects of ukiyo-e, which had primarily focused on courtesans and actors, to include landscapes, plants, and animals.

keyAchievements.title: Prolific Output
keyAchievements.description: Working tirelessly from childhood until his death, he produced over 30,000 paintings, sketches, woodblock prints, and images for picture books.

keyAchievements.title: Innovative Composition and Depiction Skills
keyAchievements.description: He is considered one of the greatest masters in art history due to his innovative compositions and outstanding depictive skills.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of Hokusai Manga
keyAchievements.description: He produced Hokusai Manga, containing thousands of drawings, which immediately became a huge success and was also used as a drawing manual.

keyAchievements.title: Experimentation with Western Perspective
keyAchievements.description: Among Japanese artists, he early on experimented with Western perspective, applying techniques to express depth and volume in his works.

keyAchievements.title: Influence on European Art
keyAchievements.description: Amid the wave of Japonisme that swept across Europe in the late 19th century, he greatly influenced Impressionist painters such as Vincent van Gogh and Claude Monet.

keyAchievements.title: Production of One Hundred Views of Mount Fuji
keyAchievements.description: He produced another important series, One Hundred Views of Mount Fuji, evaluated as "the greatest masterpiece among his landscape collections."

faq.question: Who was Katsushika Hokusai?
faq.answer: He was a Japanese ukiyo-e painter and printmaker of the Edo period, who played a crucial role in expanding the subjects of ukiyo-e to landscapes, plants, and animals.

faq.question: What is Hokusai's most famous work?
faq.answer: The Great Wave off Kanagawa, included in his ukiyo-e series Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji, is his most iconic work.

faq.question: What influence did Hokusai have on Western art?
faq.answer: Amid the wave of Japonisme that swept across Europe in the late 19th century, he greatly influenced Impressionist painters such as Vincent van Gogh and Claude Monet.

faq.question: How many names did Hokusai use in his lifetime?
faq.answer: He is known to have used at least 30 names during his lifetime, closely linked to changes in his artwork and style.

faq.question: When did Hokusai die?
faq.answer: He died on May 10, 1849, showing a passion for creating better works even on his deathbed.

timeline.event: Born at York House near the Strand in London.
Entered Trinity College, Cambridge.
Started his legal career by enrolling at Gray's Inn.
Returned home after his father's death.
Elected as a Member of Parliament for Bossiney in Cornwall.
Became an outer barrister.
Became a bencher, a senior member of the Inn.
Elected as a Reader, a law lecturer of the Inn.
Became a clerk of the Star Chamber.
Acted as a secret advisor to Robert Devereux, Earl of Essex.
Wrote Observations on a Libel, refuting the Jesuit Robert Parsons.
Elected as a Member of Parliament for Middlesex.
Became Queen's Counsel after being named legal advisor to Elizabeth I.
Arrested for failing to pay off debts.
Participated in the interrogation of the Earl of Essex for treason.
Received a knighthood as a Knight Bachelor.
Married Alice Barnham, daughter of a London alderman.
Published The Advancement of Learning.
Took the post of Solicitor General.
Officially served as a clerk of the Star Chamber.
Became Attorney General.
Prosecuted Robert Carr, Earl of Somerset, and his wife Frances Howard for murder, leading to a guilty verdict.
Sat as a regent for a month.
Appointed Lord Chancellor and became Baron Verulam.
Published Novum Organum.
Elevated to Viscount St Albans.
Impeached by Parliament on charges of bribery and stripped of all public offices and titles.
Died of pneumonia.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of Modern Empiricism and Laying the Groundwork for the Scientific Revolution
keyAchievements.description: He researched natural philosophy and led the development of the scientific method, laying the groundwork for the later Scientific Revolution, and is evaluated alongside Descartes as a pioneer of modern philosophy.

keyAchievements.title: Advocating Empiricist Philosophy and Writing Novum Organum
keyAchievements.description: He argued for empiricism, stating that the nature of the natural world could be revealed through inductive reasoning, and wrote Novum Organum, which criticized Aristotle's methodology and advocated empiricism.

keyAchievements.title: Publishing The Advancement of Learning
keyAchievements.description: In The Advancement of Learning, he classified the system of knowledge and preached the importance of inductive observation for future pursuits of truth.

keyAchievements.title: Famous Maxim 'Knowledge is Power'
keyAchievements.description: He is well known for the phrase "Knowledge is power" (scientia est potentia).

keyAchievements.title: Proposing a Library Classification System
keyAchievements.description: He patronized libraries and proposed a classification system dividing books into three categories: history, poetry, and philosophy.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of the Act of Union
keyAchievements.description: Arguing for the annexation of Scotland, he was evaluated as a pioneer of the Act of Union that later laid the foundation for the creation of the Kingdom of Great Britain.

faq.question: How is Francis Bacon evaluated?
faq.answer: He is evaluated as the founder of modern empiricism and, alongside Descartes, a pioneer of modern philosophy, leading the development of the scientific method to lay the groundwork for the Scientific Revolution.

faq.question: What are Francis Bacon's representative philosophical claims?
faq.answer: He advocated empiricism, claiming that the nature of the natural world could be revealed through inductive reasoning, and proposed a method of arriving at truth through careful observation and experimentation by eliminating preconceptions.

faq.question: What are Francis Bacon's major works?
faq.answer: His major works include Novum Organum, which advocated empiricism, and The Advancement of Learning, which classified the system of knowledge and preached the importance of inductive observation.

faq.question: What famous quote did Francis Bacon leave?
faq.answer: He is well known for the phrase "Knowledge is power" (scientia est potentia).

faq.question: What public offices did Francis Bacon hold?
faq.answer: He served as Attorney General and Lord Chancellor of England during the reign of James I, and at that time, the Lord Chancellor doubled as the Prime Minister.

timeline.event: Born in Majae, Chobu-myeon, Gwangju-bu, Gyeonggi-do.
After his father Jeong Jae-won resigned from his government post and returned to his rural home, he named Jeong Yak-yong's childhood name Gwinong.
Married Pungsan Hong, the daughter of military official Hong Hwa-bo.
His father Jeong Jae-won became the assistant secretary of the Board of Taxation and rented a house in Seoul, where he met Lee Ga-hwan and Lee Seung-hun.
Participated in the Western Learning doctrine training session at Jueosa Temple and Cheonjinam Hermitage in Yangju, Gyeonggi-do, led by Gwon Cheol-sin.
Passed the Jeungwangsi examination celebrating the investiture of the Crown Prince and became a Saengwon (licentiate) through the Hoesi examination.
Heard an explanation of Catholic doctrines from Lee Byeok, the brother-in-law of his eldest brother Jeong Yak-hyeon.
The Myeongnyebang Incident occurred, where the Myeongnyebang community run by Lee Seung-hun was discovered and he was dragged to the Board of Punishments.
King Jeongjo defined Catholicism as an evil cult and issued a ban, and ordered the search and burning of Western Learning books related to Catholicism.
Passed the highest-level state examination at the age of 27 and entered government service.
When the Jinsan Incident occurred, where Yun Ji-chung held his mother's funeral in a Catholic manner in Jinsan, Jeolla-do, he was exiled to Haemi, Seosan, but was released after 11 days.
Participated in the design of Suwon Hwaseong Fortress and utilized the Geojunggi (traditional crane).
His father Jeong Jae-won passed away.
Lectured at Seonggyungwan and patrolled Yeoncheon, Saknyeong, etc. as a secret royal inspector of Gyeonggi-do.
Demoted to Geumjeong Chalbang (station master) due to the Eulmyo Persecution incident.
Due to suspicions raised by Busagwa Bak Jang-seol, Lee Seung-hun was demoted to Yesan, Lee Ga-hwan to Chungju Moksa, and Jeong Yak-yong to Hongju Geumjeong Chalbang.
While serving as the Third Minister of the Board of Punishments, he was impeached by his older brother Jeong Yak-jeon, so he submitted a "Jamyeongso" (petition of self-vindication) and resigned.
While living in retirement in his hometown, he heard the news of King Jeongjo's death and went up to Seoul.
Queen Dowager Jeongsun issued an order suppressing Catholicism and began a purge of the Namin faction.
Arrested and imprisoned, then his sentence was reduced to exile and he was released.
Began 18 years of exile in places like Janggi, Gyeongsang-do, and Gangjin, Jeolla-do.
His son Jeong Nong-jang (Nong-a) died of smallpox.
His exile was lifted and he rose to the position of Seungji (Royal Secretary).
Returned to his hometown.
While in exile in Gangjin-gun, Jeolla-do, he conceived the Jeongjeon-ron (well-field system) to improve land inequality.
Passed away at his home in Mahyeon-ri.
Posthumously promoted to Jeongheon Daebu Gyujanggak Jehak and granted the posthumous title 'Mundo'.

keyAchievements.title: Extensive Writing Activities
keyAchievements.description: Known as the person who wrote the most books since the invention of Chinese characters, authoring over 500 volumes.

keyAchievements.title: Writing "Mokminsimseo"
keyAchievements.description: A book that details the principles and mindset of local magistrates who govern the people.

keyAchievements.title: Writing "Heumheumsinseo"
keyAchievements.description: A book detailing the principles and norms of judgments, punishments, and prison administration based on actual cases he investigated while serving as the magistrate of Goksan.

keyAchievements.title: Writing "Gyeongseyupyo"
keyAchievements.description: A book discussing appropriate and standard institutional regulations for state management.

keyAchievements.title: Writing "Magwahoetong"
keyAchievements.description: Authored the measles treatment book "Magwahoetong" based on Lee Heon-gil's "Majingibang," saving the lives of countless Joseon people.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of Geojunggi
keyAchievements.description: Created the pulley-and-windlass system for lifting heavy objects and invented the Geojunggi (traditional crane) for use during the construction of Suwon Hwaseong Fortress.

keyAchievements.title: Compilation of Silhak
keyAchievements.description: Synthesized the Silhak (Practical Learning) ideology handed down through Yu Hyeong-won and Seongho Yi Ik into a comprehensive system.

keyAchievements.title: Conception of Jeongjeon-ron
keyAchievements.description: Conceived the Jeongjeon-ron (well-field system) to improve land inequality through the free distribution of land and communal labor-communal distribution.

keyAchievements.title: Selected as a UNESCO World Commemorative Figure
keyAchievements.description: Selected as a UNESCO World Commemorative Figure in 2012 to commemorate the 250th anniversary of his birth.

faq.question: When was Jeong Yak-yong born and when did he die?
faq.answer: Jeong Yak-yong was born on August 5, 1762, and passed away on April 7, 1836.

faq.question: What are the major works of Jeong Yak-yong?
faq.answer: Jeong Yak-yong's three major works are "Mokminsimseo", "Heumheumsinseo", and "Gyeongseyupyo".

faq.question: What was Jeong Yak-yong's relationship with Catholicism?
faq.answer: Jeong Yak-yong was once deeply engrossed in Catholicism but apostatized after the Sinhae Persecution (1791), and actively participated in searching for Catholic believers during the Sinyu Persecution (1801).

faq.question: What did Jeong Yak-yong do during his exile?
faq.answer: During his 18-year exile, Jeong Yak-yong authored extensive writings, including "Mokminsimseo" and "Gyeongseyupyo".

faq.question: How did Jeong Yak-yong contribute to the construction of Suwon Hwaseong Fortress?
faq.answer: During the construction of Suwon Hwaseong Fortress, Jeong Yak-yong created a pulley-and-windlass system for lifting heavy objects and invented the Geojunggi (traditional crane) to utilize it.

faq.question: What did Jeong Yak-yong's ideology aim for?
faq.answer: Jeong Yak-yong aimed to establish Silhak of statecraft and practical use, correct distorted theories by returning to the essence of Confucianism of Confucius and Mencius, and build a welfare state that cares for the socially and economically weak.

faq.question: Was Jeong Yak-yong selected as a UNESCO World Commemorative Figure?
faq.answer: Yes, Jeong Yak-yong was selected as a UNESCO World Commemorative Figure in 2012, marking the 250th anniversary of his birth.

timeline.event: Born in Clermont-Ferrand, France, to Etienne Pascal, a tax official in Rouen
Discovered by himself that the sum of the interior angles of a triangle is 180 degrees
Discovered Pascal's triangle
Participated in the main regular meetings of a group of French mathematicians
Proved Pascal's theorem, which is the foundation of projective geometry
Derived 400 propositions using Pascal's theorem
Invented the Pascaline, the first calculator
Established Pascal's law, which laid the foundation for hydrostatics, through an experiment using a mercury column
Conducted a vacuum experiment using various shaped tubes in Rouen
His experiments were conducted in Paris by Mersenne and other scientists
Published 'Discours sur le vide' (Thoughts on the Vacuum)
Moved from Rouen to Paris due to failing health
Debated with Descartes on the existence of a vacuum
Wrote a paper titled 'Experiences nouvelles touchant le vide' (New Experiments with the Vacuum)
Performed 'Pascal's experiment' observing the change in the height of a mercury column with altitude at Puy de Dome
Wrote a paper summarizing the results of the Puy de Dome experiment
Suspended his mathematics and science research to focus his mind on religious contemplation
Explained the law of pressure (Pascal's principle) in 'Traitez de l'equilibre des liqueurs' (Treatises on the Equilibrium of Liquids)
Involved in a carriage accident
Wrote a confession of faith claiming to have heard a voice from heaven and became deeply involved in Christian theology
Suffered mentally while battling headaches
Gave his house to a poor family suffering from smallpox and moved into his sister's house
Died from convulsions
After his death, 'Traitez de l'equilibre des liqueurs' was published by Perier

keyAchievements.title: Pascal's triangle
keyAchievements.description: A geometric arrangement of binomial coefficients in the shape of a triangle in mathematics.

keyAchievements.title: Pascal's theorem
keyAchievements.description: A theorem that is the foundation of projective geometry, which he proved at the age of 16.

keyAchievements.title: Pascaline (Pascal's calculator)
keyAchievements.description: The world's first calculator, invented at the age of 19, capable of addition and subtraction.

keyAchievements.title: Pascal's principle
keyAchievements.description: A law stating that if pressure is applied to a portion of a confined, incompressible fluid, that pressure is transmitted equally to all parts of the fluid.

keyAchievements.title: Laid the foundation for probability theory
keyAchievements.description: Co-founded the foundation of probability theory through correspondence with Fermat.

keyAchievements.title: Cycloid research
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to the development of mathematics by studying the cycloid to forget his headaches.

keyAchievements.title: Authored 'Pensees'
keyAchievements.description: An unfinished book containing his Christian apologetics, including the famous saying, 'Man is a thinking reed.'

keyAchievements.title: Authored 'Provincial Letters'
keyAchievements.description: A book written to defend Jansenist clergy oppressed by the Jesuits, evaluated as the starting point of modern French prose.

faq.question: Who was Blaise Pascal?
faq.answer: A French psychologist, mathematician, scientist, theologian, physicist, inventor, writer, philosopher, and statistician who invested a lot of time especially in philosophy and theology.

faq.question: What is the Pascal calculator?
faq.answer: The world's first calculator invented at the age of 19 to help his accountant father, capable only of addition and subtraction.

faq.question: What is Pascal's principle?
faq.answer: A law stating that if pressure is applied to a portion of a confined, incompressible fluid, that pressure is transmitted equally to all parts of the fluid.

faq.question: What is Pascal's famous saying?
faq.answer: He left the famous saying, 'Man is a thinking reed.'

faq.question: Why did Pascal stop his mathematics and science research?
faq.answer: At the age of 27, he focused his mind on religious contemplation and thus suspended his mathematics and science research.

timeline.event: Born on June 23
Started majoring in mathematics at the University of Cambridge
Took physicist Eddington's scientific methodology class and proved the central limit theorem
Left for Princeton University after providing a negative proof to the decidability problem
Finished studies at Princeton University and returned to Cambridge
Joined the Government Code and Cypher School (GCCS) during World War II and started cryptography research
Devised the Turing machine; pushed for the creation of a mathematics department to build an electronic calculator at the National Physical Laboratory (NPL) on October 1
Awarded the Officer of the Order of the British Empire (OBE)
Published a paper titled 'Can Machines Think?' in the philosophy journal 'Mind'
Arrested and convicted by British police on charges of homosexuality
Published a paper on the Riemann zeta function
Died of potassium cyanide poisoning on June 7
Queen Elizabeth II pardoned Turing's homosexuality conviction on December 24

keyAchievements.title: Devised the Turing machine
keyAchievements.description: Formalized the concepts of algorithm and computation through an abstract model called the Turing machine, making a profound contribution to the development of computer science.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed the Turing test
keyAchievements.description: Proposed an experiment to evaluate how human-like a machine's answers are with the proposition 'Can machines think?', achieving great success.

keyAchievements.title: Cracked the Enigma code
keyAchievements.description: Successfully cracked the German codes during World War II by introducing a code-breaking machine called the Bombe.

keyAchievements.title: Proved the central limit theorem
keyAchievements.description: Proved it in 1933 without knowing that Finnish mathematician Jarl Waldemar Lindeberg had already proved it in 1922.

keyAchievements.title: Negative proof of the decidability problem
keyAchievements.description: Provided a negative proof to the decidability problem in 1936.

faq.question: What fields was Alan Turing a pioneer in?
faq.answer: He was an English computer scientist, mathematician, cryptanalyst, logician, and a pioneering figure in computer science.

faq.question: What is the purpose of the Turing Award?
faq.answer: It is an annual prize given by the ACM to those who have made significant contributions to computer science, established in 1966 to honor Turing's contributions.

faq.question: Why is Alan Turing called the 'father of computer science'?
faq.answer: He is called the 'father of computer science' because he made profound contributions to theoretical computer science and artificial intelligence.

faq.question: When was Alan Turing pardoned for his homosexuality conviction?
faq.answer: Queen Elizabeth II pardoned his homosexuality conviction on December 24, 2013, 59 years after his death.

timeline.event: Born in Alcala de Henares, a university town in Madrid
Followed an Italian cardinal to Rome, became a soldier, and participated in the Battle of Lepanto, where he suffered an injury that left his left hand crippled
Captured by pirates and lived as a slave in Algiers for 5 years
Paid his ransom with the help of the Trinitarian Order, returned to Madrid, and began to write
Published the novel 'La Galatea', but it did not gain popularity
Published Part 1 of 'Don Quixote', which gained immense popularity
Completed Part 2 of 'Don Quixote'
Passed away at the age of 69

keyAchievements.title: Don Quixote
keyAchievements.description: A work evaluated by many as the first modern novel in Europe.

keyAchievements.title: La Galatea
keyAchievements.description: Published the novel 'La Galatea', but it did not attract much popularity.

faq.question: What is Miguel de Cervantes's nickname?
faq.answer: He has the nickname 'Prince of Wits' (el Príncipe de los Ingenios).

faq.question: Where was Miguel de Cervantes born?
faq.answer: He was born in Alcala de Henares, a university town in Madrid.

faq.question: What were Miguel de Cervantes's main professions?
faq.answer: He was a novelist, poet, and playwright.

faq.question: How are Miguel de Cervantes's works evaluated?
faq.answer: His works are mentioned among the most important masterpieces in all of literature, and 'Don Quixote' in particular is evaluated as the first modern novel in Europe.

timeline.event: Born
Created the masterpieces 'Inochi futatsu no naka ni ikitaru sakura kana' and 'Kumo oriori hito o yasumuru tsukimi kana'
Created the masterpiece 'Furuike ya kawazu tobikomu mizu no oto' (Old pond / a frog jumps in / sound of water)
Died (November 28, lunar October 12)

keyAchievements.title: Recognition of Haikai-no-renga style
keyAchievements.description: His works were recognized during his lifetime in the collaborative haikai-no-renga style.

keyAchievements.title: Master of Haiku
keyAchievements.description: Today, he is considered a master of haiku, which was called hokku during his time.

keyAchievements.title: Internationally renowned poet
keyAchievements.description: His poetry is internationally renowned, and many of his poems in Japan are reproduced at monuments or historical sites.

keyAchievements.title: Fame across Japan
keyAchievements.description: He entered the world of poetry at a young age, and by integrating himself into the intellectual landscape of Edo, he became famous throughout Japan.

keyAchievements.title: Inspiration through travel
keyAchievements.description: He wandered throughout Japan, including the north, east, and west, to gain inspiration for his works.

keyAchievements.title: Poetry based on experiences of nature
keyAchievements.description: His poetry was influenced by his direct experiences of the world around him, condensing emotions about landscapes into simple elements.

faq.question: Who was Matsuo Basho?
faq.answer: Matsuo Basho, born in 1644 and died on November 28, 1694 (lunar October 12), was a poet of the early Edo period. His childhood name was Kinsaku, and he was also known as Matsuo Chuemon Munefusa.

faq.question: What are Matsuo Basho's major achievements?
faq.answer: During his lifetime, his works were recognized in the collaborative haikai-no-renga style, and today he is considered a master of haiku. His poetry is internationally renowned and reproduced at monuments and historical sites across Japan.

faq.question: What are Matsuo Basho's representative works?
faq.answer: Representative works include 'Inochi futatsu no naka ni ikitaru sakura kana' and 'Kumo oriori hito o yasumuru tsukimi kana' from 1685, and 'Furuike ya kawazu tobikomu mizu no oto' from 1686.

faq.question: How did Matsuo Basho conduct his literary activities?
faq.answer: While making a living as a teacher, he distanced himself from the social and urban life where literary circles were based, wandering throughout Japan to gain inspiration. His poetry was influenced by direct experiences of the surrounding world, condensing his emotions about landscapes.

faq.question: Which of his works did Matsuo Basho consider most important?
faq.answer: He believed his best work lay in participating in and leading renku, and considered the connection with haikai verses more important than hokku.

timeline.event: Born to Pepin the Short and Bertrada of Laon in Herstal, Wallonia, Frankish Kingdom, near Liege, Belgium.
His father, Pepin the Short, became King of the Franks with the approval of Pope Zachary.
Pope Stephen II bestowed the title of King on Pepin and his sons Charles and Carloman.
Succeeded to the throne after the death of his father Pepin the Short, taking over Austrasia and the northern regions.
Became sole ruler after the death of his brother Carloman I.
Began attacking the Saxons.
Destroyed and annexed the Lombard Kingdom in northern Italy.
Attended the Easter festival in Rome with the Pope and reaffirmed the promise to transfer the Papal States.
Suffered a crushing defeat by the Basques at the Battle of Roncevaux Pass during an expedition to the Iberian Peninsula.
Visited Rome, had his two sons Pepin and Louis consecrated, and bestowed upon them the crowns of Lombardy and Aquitaine respectively.
His wife Hildegard and mother Bertrada of Laon passed away successively, and he remarried Fastrada.
Conquered Thuringia and Bavaria, and deposed Duke Tassilo III of Bavaria.
Soundly defeated the Avars along the Danube River and drove them beyond the borders.
Built a palace and royal church in Aachen, northwestern Germany, making it the de facto capital.
Pope Leo III, attacked by opponents, fled to Charlemagne's court to seek assistance.
Was crowned Emperor of the Romans by Pope Leo III in St. Peter's Basilica.
Louis the Pious captured Barcelona.
The Avar Khagan Zodan visited Aachen, swore fealty to Charlemagne, and converted to Christianity.
Croatian chiefs, including Viseslav, voluntarily surrendered.
Eastern Roman Emperor Michael I Rangabe recognized Charlemagne as Emperor.
Declared his surviving son Louis I the Pious as the legitimate successor at the court in Aachen and crowned him co-emperor.
Died of complications from pleurisy and depression at the Palace of Aachen.
Pope Paschal III canonized Charlemagne as a saint at the request of Frederick I.

keyAchievements.title: Expansion of the Frankish Kingdom
keyAchievements.description: Greatly expanded the Frankish Kingdom by conquering most of western and central Europe, achieving the political unification of Western Europe.

keyAchievements.title: Crowned Emperor of the Romans
keyAchievements.description: Received the title of Emperor of the Romans from Pope Leo III in December 800, politically and religiously unifying most of Western Europe for the first time since the Roman Empire.

keyAchievements.title: Carolingian Renaissance
keyAchievements.description: Initiated the Carolingian Renaissance by greatly developing arts, religion, and culture through the church during his reign.

keyAchievements.title: Codification of laws and centralization
keyAchievements.description: Codified the laws of various tribes to rule over vast territories and enabled centralized rule by dispatching officials from the center.

keyAchievements.title: Spread and integration of Christianity
keyAchievements.description: Sought ideological unity by converting conquered tribes to Catholicism and supported proselytization by dispatching missionaries.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of cultural and educational institutions
keyAchievements.description: Led a revival of learning and arts by building a palace and royal church in Aachen, and founding a royal library and palace school.

faq.question: When was Charlemagne born?
faq.answer: There are theories that Charlemagne was born on April 2 of 740 or 742, or in 747.

faq.question: Why is Charlemagne called the 'Father of Europe'?
faq.answer: He is called the 'Father of Europe' because the Carolingian Renaissance laid the foundation for the current identity of Europe.

faq.question: When did Charlemagne become Emperor of the Romans?
faq.answer: Charlemagne received the title of Emperor of the Romans from Pope Leo III in December 800.

faq.question: How tall was Charlemagne?
faq.answer: According to Einhard's records and the results of a tomb excavation in 1861, Charlemagne was about 190 to 191 cm tall.

faq.question: What languages did Charlemagne speak?
faq.answer: In addition to his mother tongue, he was fluent in Latin and could also speak some Greek.

faq.question: Where was Charlemagne born?
faq.answer: Charlemagne was born in Herstal, Wallonia, Frankish Kingdom, near Liege, Belgium.

timeline.event: Born on September 22 as the third of four children to James Faraday and Margaret in Newington.
Became an apprentice to George Riebau, a bookseller, at the age of 13.
Signed a seven-year contract as George Riebau's apprentice on August 7.
Read Jane Marcet's 'Conversations on Chemistry' and the 3rd edition of the Encyclopædia Britannica by James Tytler.
Began taking notes on articles about art and science from newspapers and magazines.
Received tickets to Humphry Davy's chemistry lectures and attended them; wrote a letter asking Davy for help.
Started working at the Royal Institution in March; began a European tour as Davy's secretary and assistant on October 13.
Traveled to Naples, Italy, climbed Mount Vesuvius, and met Alessandro Volta in Pavia.
Returned to England on April 23; re-employed as superintendent and assistant of the apparatus and mineralogical collections at the Royal Institution's laboratory in May.
Married Sarah Barnard on June 12; published an article on electromagnetic rotation in 'Annals of Philosophy'.
Left the note 'Convert magnetism into electricity' in his laboratory notebook.
Successfully liquefied chlorine.
Discovered benzene; commissioned by the Royal Society to research the optical properties of glass used for telescopes.
Determined the molecular structure of naphthalene and created two sample crystals; established the 'Christmas Lectures for Children'.
Published a paper including practical guidance on chemistry in April; began optical glass experiments with a special furnace provided by the Royal Institution.
Announced the success of his optical glass research at the Bakerian Lecture of the Royal Society.
Began electromagnetic experiments on August 29 and discovered electromagnetic induction.
Began 'Electrochemical Decomposition' research at the end of the year and discovered Faraday's first law.
Experimented with various dissolved substances in a voltameter at the end of the year and discovered Faraday's second law.
Discovered that the charge on a charged conductor resides only on its exterior surface.
Published Volume 1 of 'Experimental Researches in Electricity'.
Successfully liquefied many substances including ammonia and carbon dioxide.
Met William Thomson at Cambridge University in June; discovered the Faraday effect on September 18, defining a physical relationship between magnetism and light.
Began experiments to investigate changes in diamagnetic materials influenced by magnets, and mentioned the concept of a magnetic field.
Granted residence in a house near Hampton Court along the River Thames in London at the request of Queen Victoria.
Published 'Experimental Researches in Chemistry and Physics'.

Submitted research results on gravity and electricity to the 'Philosophical Transactions' but was advised to withdraw them; gave a lecture on 'The Chemical History of a Candle' at the Royal Institution's Christmas Lectures.
Began suffering from memory loss symptoms; gave his final lecture at Christmas.
Conducted an experiment on whether the spectrum of sodium and other metals heated by a flame is affected by an electromagnetic field.
Passed away on August 25.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of Electromagnetic Induction
keyAchievements.description: Discovered the principles of electromagnetic induction, diamagnetism, and the laws of electrolysis, and invented an electromagnetic rotary device that formed the foundation of the electric motor.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of Benzene and Introduction of Chemical Terms
keyAchievements.description: Discovered benzene as a chemist, investigated the clathrate hydrate of chlorine, and introduced technical terms such as anode, cathode, electrode, and ion for the first time.

keyAchievements.title: Successful Liquefaction of Gases
keyAchievements.description: Successfully liquefied many substances such as chlorine, ammonia, and carbon dioxide, proving the existence of critical points and the interchangeability of states of matter.

keyAchievements.title: Research on Manufacturing Optical Glass
keyAchievements.description: Researched the optical properties of glass used in telescopes and developed a method to produce uniform glass without bubbles or striae.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of the Faraday Effect
keyAchievements.description: Established the fundamental relationship between a magnetic field and light rays, showing that a magnetic field can affect light, and discovered the phenomenon of the plane of polarization rotating in a magnetic field.

keyAchievements.title: Classification of Magnetic Materials
keyAchievements.description: Named diamagnetic materials that stop perpendicularly to a magnetic field and paramagnetic materials that stop parallel to it, discovering that most gases are diamagnetic while oxygen has paramagnetism.

keyAchievements.title: Introduction of the Magnetic Field Concept
keyAchievements.description: Believed that lines of magnetic force had physical meaning and first mentioned the concept of a magnetic field, where the influence from two magnetic poles varies depending on the position.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of the Faraday Cage Principle
keyAchievements.description: Discovered that the electrical charge on a charged conductor resides only on its exterior and exerts no influence on anything enclosed within it.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of the Christmas Lectures
keyAchievements.description: Created the 'Christmas Lectures for Children' in 1826 to popularize science, which has been passed down as a tradition of the Royal Institution in London.

faq.question: What was Michael Faraday's educational background?
faq.answer: Faraday received little formal or higher education during his childhood, learning only the basics of reading, writing, and arithmetic. He acquired his scientific knowledge primarily through self-study.

faq.question: Was Faraday proficient in mathematics?
faq.answer: Faraday lacked mathematical skills to the point that he could only do simple algebra and could not handle trigonometry, but he excelled in visual thinking and imagination. His theories were later mathematically reformulated by James Clerk Maxwell.

faq.question: Did Faraday pursue fame or wealth?
faq.answer: No. He was not interested in worldly wealth, refused to publish his lectures, and declined the presidency of the Royal Society as well as a knighthood. His humility and frugality stemmed from his religious beliefs.

faq.question: Did Faraday have children?
faq.answer: No. Faraday married Sarah Barnard, but they had no children throughout their lives. However, he created and participated in science lectures for children, contributing to the popularization of science.

faq.question: How did Faraday's religion influence his scientific research?
faq.answer: Faraday was a devout follower of Christian fundamentalism and a member of the Sandemanian congregation. His humility, frugality, and pure pursuit of academics were closely related to this religious background.

timeline.event: Born in Vienna, Austria.
Obtained a Ph.D. from the University of Vienna.
Married Annemarie (Anny) Bertel.
Moved to the University of Zurich.
Published the paper 'Quantization as an Eigenvalue Problem' containing the Schrödinger equation.
Succeeded Max Planck at Friedrich Wilhelm University in Berlin.
Left Germany in opposition to Nazism.
Awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics along with Paul Dirac.
Proposed the Schrödinger's cat thought experiment.
Fired in Graz after the Austrian annexation, fled to Italy, and later relocated to Dublin.
Became the Director of the School for Theoretical Physics at the Dublin Institute for Advanced Studies.
Authored 'What Is Life?'.
Became a naturalized Irish citizen.
Retired in Dublin.
Returned to Vienna.
Died of tuberculosis in Vienna at the age of 73.
The Department of Physics at Trinity College Dublin recommended removing his name from a lecture theatre considering his history of sexual abuse.

keyAchievements.title: Development of the Schrödinger equation
keyAchievements.description: Developed an equation providing a way to calculate a system's wave function and how it changes dynamically over time, thereby developing the fundamental results of quantum mechanics.

keyAchievements.title: Nobel Prize in Physics
keyAchievements.description: Awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1933 along with Paul Dirac for the formulation of the Schrödinger equation.

keyAchievements.title: Authored 'What Is Life?'
keyAchievements.description: Viewed life phenomena from the perspective of physics and addressed the problem of genetics, inspiring the discovery of the DNA double helix structure.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed Schrödinger's cat thought experiment
keyAchievements.description: A famous thought experiment designed to mock the Copenhagen interpretation of quantum mechanics, and one of his most enduring legacies.

keyAchievements.title: Research on unified field theory
keyAchievements.description: Made several attempts to construct a unified field theory integrating gravity, electromagnetism, and nuclear forces.

keyAchievements.title: Research on color theory
keyAchievements.description: Showed a strong interest in psychology, particularly color perception and colorimetry, and published a series of papers in this field.

faq.question: For what achievement did Erwin Schrödinger win the Nobel Prize?
faq.answer: He was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1933 along with Paul Dirac for the formulation of the Schrödinger equation.

faq.question: What is Schrödinger's cat?
faq.answer: It is a famous thought experiment designed to mock the Copenhagen interpretation of quantum mechanics.

faq.question: What books did Schrödinger author?
faq.answer: He authored 'What Is Life?', which includes discussions on the genetic code of living organisms and negentropy.

faq.question: When and where was Schrödinger born and when did he die?
faq.answer: He was born in Vienna, Austria on August 12, 1887, and died of tuberculosis in Vienna on January 4, 1961, at the age of 73.

faq.question: Why did Schrödinger leave Germany?
faq.answer: He left Germany in 1933 because he despised the anti-Semitism of the Nazis.

timeline.event: Birth of Tran Hung Dao, an imperial prince and general of the Tran dynasty in Great Viet
When an envoy of the Mongol general Uriyangkhadai demanded safe passage through Vietnam, Tran Hung Dao was assigned the duty of border defense
When Kublai Khan's son Toghon invaded Vietnam with an army of 500,000, Nhan Tong appointed Tran Hung Dao as the Commander-in-Chief to lead the army
The army led by Tran Hung Dao fought against the Mongol forces, but the capital Thang Long was captured, and the retired emperor and Nhan Tong fled to Thanh Hoa
Tran Hung Dao compiled the 'Binh thu yeu luoc' (Summary of Military Tactics) and wrote the 'Hich tuong si' (Proclamation to the Officers) to boost the troops' morale
The army led by Tran Hung Dao recaptured Thang Long and forced Toghon's Mongol army to retreat
When the Yuan dynasty's army invaded again, Tran Hung Dao withdrew his troops from Thang Long and annihilated the Yuan fleet at the Bach Dang River (Battle of Bach Dang)
Tran Hung Dao died, leaving the last words: 'Unite the army like parents and children, treat the people generously, and use that strength to achieve great endeavors'

keyAchievements.title: Repelling Mongol Invasions
keyAchievements.description: Successfully repelled massive invasions by the Yuan dynasty twice, in 1285 and 1287, safeguarding the independence of Vietnam.

keyAchievements.title: Military Strategy and Morale Boosting
keyAchievements.description: Authored the 'Binh thu yeu luoc' and the 'Hich tuong si' to boost the morale of his soldiers and provide effective military strategies.

keyAchievements.title: Victory at the Battle of Bach Dang
keyAchievements.description: Achieved a decisive victory in the Battle of Bach Dang in 1287 by using the strategy of planting wooden stakes in the riverbed to annihilate the Yuan fleet.

faq.question: What is Tran Hung Dao's real name?
faq.answer: Tran Hung Dao's real name is Tran Quoc Tuan, and Hung Dao is a title bestowed upon him later to praise his achievements.

faq.question: What books did Tran Hung Dao write?
faq.answer: His works include 'Binh thu yeu luoc' (Summary of Military Tactics) and 'Van Kiep tong bi truyen thu' (Esoteric Manual of Van Kiep).

faq.question: What is a famous statement made by Tran Hung Dao?
faq.answer: During the Mongol invasion in 1285, he showed his will to fight by saying, 'If Your Majesty wishes to surrender, please cut off my head first'.

timeline.event: Born to a Protestant officer father in Montbeliard, Württemberg
Enrolled in the Caroline Academy (Karlsschule) in Stuttgart and spent 4 years

keyAchievements.title: Founded comparative anatomy
keyAchievements.description: Earned the title of the 'Father of Comparative Anatomy' for establishing the foundation of comparative anatomy by systematically comparing the anatomical structures of various animals.

keyAchievements.title: Founded vertebrate paleontology
keyAchievements.description: Established vertebrate paleontology by studying fossil bones and proposing the theory of extinction.

keyAchievements.title: Developed the principle of correlation of parts
keyAchievements.description: Proposed the 'principle of correlation of parts', stating that the shape and function of each organ in an organism are closely linked to the overall structure and lifestyle of the animal.

faq.question: What is Georges Cuvier's most famous achievement?
faq.answer: He is the French zoologist known for founding comparative anatomy and paleontology.

faq.question: What is catastrophism advocated by Cuvier?
faq.answer: It is the theory that sudden, widespread catastrophic events like massive floods occurred multiple times in Earth's past history, causing the extinction of species.

faq.question: What was Cuvier's view on evolution?
faq.answer: He opposed the theory of evolution. According to his principle of correlation of parts, an organism's structure is perfectly adapted to its lifestyle, so he believed that changes in some parts would break the overall harmony, making survival impossible.

faq.question: How did Cuvier prove that extinction occurred?
faq.answer: He proved that extinction was a real phenomenon by demonstrating that fossil elephant bones, like mammoths and mastodons, belonged to different species than living elephants.

faq.question: 조르주 퀴비에는 파리 박물관에서 어떻게 교수가 되었나요?
faq.answer: 그는 생틸르의 소개로 파리 박물관의 교수가 되었습니다.

timeline.event: Born on the island of Samos
Traveled to various countries including Egypt, Phoenicia, and Chaldea after the age of 18 (unconfirmed)
Moved to Croton in southern Italy and founded a religious organization and philosophical school
With the rise of democratic politics in Croton, the Pythagorean order was violently suppressed

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of the Pythagorean Theorem
keyAchievements.description: Proved the geometric theorem that the square of the hypotenuse is equal to the sum of the squares of the other two sides in a right-angled triangle.

keyAchievements.title: Belief that all things are numbers
keyAchievements.description: Considered numbers to be the fundamental principle of the universe and emphasized their mathematical relationships and ratios.

keyAchievements.title: Mathematical theory of music
keyAchievements.description: Discovered that harmonious musical scales form simple numerical ratios and applied this to the movement of the universe.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Pythagorean School
keyAchievements.description: Founded an exclusive religious and philosophical community centered around asceticism and mathematical exploration in Croton, southern Italy.

keyAchievements.title: Development of mathematical deductive methods
keyAchievements.description: Elevated the practicality of mathematics to the realm of logical deduction and proof.

faq.question: Who was Pythagoras?
faq.answer: An ancient Greek philosopher and mathematician from Ionia, the founder of the Pythagorean school, widely known for discovering the Pythagorean theorem.

faq.question: Did Pythagoras actually discover the Pythagorean theorem?
faq.answer: Although the theorem bears his name, it is unclear whether he himself proved it, and the content of the theorem was already known to the Babylonians.

faq.question: What were the main teachings of the Pythagorean school?
faq.answer: They believed in the transmigration of souls (metempsychosis), practiced asceticism such as vegetarianism, and sought the fundamental harmony of the universe in numbers and their relationships.

faq.question: Why is it difficult to distinguish between the achievements of Pythagoras and those of his followers?
faq.answer: Because the followers of the Pythagorean school attributed all discoveries to the founder Pythagoras, and kept their knowledge strictly secret.

faq.question: 피타고라스는 어디에서 학파를 설립했나요?
faq.answer: 피타고라스는 남부 이탈리아의 크로토네로 이동하여 종교적인 학파를 세웠습니다.

timeline.event: Born into slavery in Holly Springs, Mississippi
Emancipation Proclamation issued, freeing her family
Parents and younger brother die of yellow fever; Ida takes responsibility for her siblings
Forcibly removed from a first-class train car; files a lawsuit against the railroad company
Wins the lawsuit at the local court
The Tennessee Supreme Court overturns the ruling in favor of the railroad company
A friend's lynching incident occurs; she begins a full-scale anti-lynching campaign in 'The Free Speech and Headlight'
Her newspaper office is destroyed by a white mob; she relocates to Chicago
Publishes the anti-lynching pamphlet 'Southern Horrors: Lynch Law in All Its Phases'
Publishes another anti-lynching pamphlet 'The Red Record'
Marries Ferdinand L. Barnett
Participates in the founding of the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP)

keyAchievements.title: Anti-lynching Journalism
keyAchievements.description: Through investigative journalism, she systematically investigated and exposed the reality that the lynching of Black people in the South was often used to eliminate Black people who achieved economic success, rather than to punish criminals.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of Anti-lynching Pamphlets
keyAchievements.description: Published pamphlets containing the realities of lynching, such as 'Southern Horrors' (1892) and 'The Red Record' (1895), drawing national and international attention to the issue.

keyAchievements.title: Founding of Civil Rights Organizations
keyAchievements.description: Participated in the founding of the National Association of Colored Women (NACW) in 1896 and the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP) in 1909.

faq.question: What kind of environment was Ida B. Wells born into?
faq.answer: She was born into slavery in Holly Springs, Mississippi in 1862, but was freed the following year by the Emancipation Proclamation.

faq.question: What motivated Ida B. Wells to start her anti-lynching campaign?
faq.answer: In 1892, a close friend and two other Black men were lynched by a white mob out of economic jealousy over their successful grocery store. This incident prompted her to begin a full-scale anti-lynching campaign.

faq.question: What challenges did Ida B. Wells face because of her anti-lynching campaign?
faq.answer: Her newspaper office in Memphis was destroyed by a white mob, and she received numerous death threats, eventually forcing her to leave the South and relocate to Chicago.

faq.question: What is the Alpha Suffrage Club?
faq.answer: It is the first Black women's suffrage organization in Illinois, founded by Ida B. Wells in Chicago in 1913.

timeline.event: Born in Leipzig
Obtained a bachelor's degree in philosophy from the University of Leipzig
Obtained a master's degree in philosophy
The University of Leipzig refused his doctoral application in law due to his young age
Obtained a doctorate in law and was granted the right to practice from the University of Altdorf
Served the Elector of Mainz
Stayed in Paris, expanding his mathematical and scientific knowledge
Devised the binary number system
Entered the service of the House of Hanover as librarian and political advisor
Developed infinitesimal calculus
Discovered the concept of kinetic energy
Traveled through Germany, Austria, and Italy to write the history of the House of Brunswick
Proposed the idea of establishing an academy to the Elector of Brandenburg, leading to the creation of the Prussian Academy of Sciences
The Royal Society recognized Newton's priority in inventing calculus, declaring Leibniz a plagiarist
Wrote 'Monadology' based on his philosophical thoughts
George Louis, Elector of Hanover, ascended the British throne as George I, but Leibniz was forbidden from going to London
Died in Hanover; his funeral was held quietly
유럽 연합이 나올 것이라는 예상을 번복함.
사망함.
《인간오성신론》이 출판됨.

keyAchievements.title: Development of Infinitesimal Calculus
keyAchievements.description: Developed infinitesimal calculus independently of Isaac Newton, and the mathematical notation he devised is the one mainly used today.

keyAchievements.title: Devised the Binary Number System
keyAchievements.description: Refined the binary number system, which forms the basis of modern computer architecture.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of a Mechanical Calculator
keyAchievements.description: Invented the stepped reckoner, the first mechanical calculator capable of performing all four arithmetic operations.

keyAchievements.title: Concept of Kinetic Energy (Vis Viva)
keyAchievements.description: Discovered the concept of 'vis viva' (living force), anticipating the law of conservation of energy.

keyAchievements.title: Concept of Monads
keyAchievements.description: In philosophy, he proposed the concept of 'monads', explaining the universe as being composed of simple, immaterial substances.

keyAchievements.title: Principle of Sufficient Reason
keyAchievements.description: Proposed the principle of sufficient reason, stating that 'nothing happens without a reason'.

keyAchievements.title: Law of Continuity
keyAchievements.description: Proposed the law of continuity, stating that 'nature makes no leaps'.

keyAchievements.title: Concept of the 'Best of All Possible Worlds'
keyAchievements.description: Optimistically argued that our universe is the 'best of all possible worlds' created by God.

keyAchievements.title: 도서관학 발전
keyAchievements.description: 독일 하노버와 볼펜뷔텔의 도서관에서 사서로 일하며 도서관학의 창시자 중 한 명으로, 새로운 도서관 건물을 설계하고 독자적인 색인 체계를 고안했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 베를린 학사원 설립
keyAchievements.description: 1700년에 베를린 학사원 설립을 직접적으로 추진했으며, 죽을 때까지 그 학회의 회장을 맡았습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 중국 철학 연구
keyAchievements.description: 『주역』의 괘가 2진법 숫자와 연관성이 있음을 주목하고, 유학적 개념을 유럽인들의 신앙에 수용시키려고 시도했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 《변신론》 저술
keyAchievements.description: 저서 《변신론》에서 세상에는 반드시 불완전함이 존재함을 '있을 수 있는 세계 중 최상의 세계'라는 용어를 통해 증명하려 했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 유럽 연맹 제안
keyAchievements.description: 1677년에 각 나라 대표가 모여 운영되는 유럽 연맹을 제안하여 유럽 연합의 출현을 예견했습니다.

faq.question: What was the calculus priority dispute?
faq.answer: A fierce dispute arose between Isaac Newton and Leibniz over who first invented calculus. Today, it is recognized that both discovered it independently.

faq.question: Why are Leibniz's mathematical notations important?
faq.answer: The notations used by Leibniz, such as the integral symbol (∫) and the derivative notation (d/dx), were intuitive and convenient, playing a significant role in the development of mathematics.

faq.question: What is Leibniz's Monadology?
faq.answer: It is a philosophy asserting that the universe is composed of simple, immaterial, soul-like entities called 'monads', which do not interact with each other but form a harmony pre-established by God.

faq.question: What role did Leibniz play in the history of computers?
faq.answer: He refined the binary number system, the basis of modern computers, and invented a mechanical calculator capable of performing all four arithmetic operations, establishing him as a pioneer of computer science.

faq.question: How did Leibniz spend his later years?
faq.answer: He suffered a great loss of reputation due to the calculus priority dispute with Newton. His employer, George I, moved to England, but Leibniz was left behind in Hanover, where he died lonely and sick with gout.

faq.question: 라이프니츠는 결혼했었나요?
faq.answer: 라이프니츠는 미혼으로 살았습니다.

faq.question: 라이프니츠의 종교는 무엇이었나요?
faq.answer: 라이프니츠의 종교는 개신교였으며, 철학적 유신론자였습니다.

faq.question: 라이프니츠는 어떤 학사원 설립에 기여했나요?
faq.answer: 라이프니츠는 1700년에 설립된 베를린 학사원의 설립을 직접적으로 추진했으며, 죽을 때까지 그 학회의 회장을 맡았습니다.

timeline.event: Proclaimed heiress of the House of Habsburg by the Pragmatic Sanction.
May 13, born as the eldest daughter of Holy Roman Emperor Charles VI and Elisabeth Christine of Brunswick-Wolfenbüttel.
Fell in love with Francis Stephen of Lorraine.
February 12, married Francis Stephen of Lorraine.
The War of the Austrian Succession broke out after the death of Charles VI.
March 13, gave birth to son Joseph.
June 25, crowned Queen of Hungary.
September 11, addressed the Hungarian Diet and obtained war funds and troops.
Signed the Treaty of Dresden, recognizing Prussia's possession of Silesia.
October 4, successfully secured the imperial throne for her husband, Francis Stephen.
The War of the Austrian Succession ended with the signing of the Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle.
The Seven Years' War broke out between Prussia and Austria.
Russia withdrew from the Seven Years' War after the death of Elizabeth and the accession of Peter III.
Military organizations were consolidated under the Court Council of War, making it the supreme decision-making body.
Peace was made through the Treaty of Hubertusburg (Pressburg in some contexts), finalizing Prussia's possession of Silesia.
After the death of her husband Francis I, her son Joseph II was crowned Holy Roman Emperor.
Freedom of dress was implemented.
Approved the love marriage of her fourth daughter, Maria Christina.
Suppression of the Jesuits was implemented.
November 29, died.

keyAchievements.title: Domestic and Military Reforms
keyAchievements.description: Promoted the modernization of Austria by carrying out domestic reforms with the appointment of Haugwitz and military reforms led by Daun.

keyAchievements.title: Diplomatic Revolution
